AgentCash in action.

remember ERC-8004?

it went live on Ethereum mainnet in January. 45,000 agents registered in the first month. the standard gives agents portable onchain identity, a reputation registry, and a validation layer. it is good infrastructure and the ecosystem needed it.

it also explicitly does not handle payments.

that's a design choice, not an oversight. the authors scoped ERC-8004 narrowly on purpose: identity, reputation, validation. how an agent actually moves money is someone else's problem. x402 is the answer the ecosystem landed on, an open protocol that lets any HTTP-accessible resource accept stablecoin payment from an agent with no intermediary and no manual rails.

what neither standard addresses is what happens when an agent pays the wrong counterparty. ERC-8004 can tell you an agent has a reputation score. x402 can settle the transaction in milliseconds. neither one checks whether the wallet on the other end is on a sanctions list before the funds move.

ampersend sits between the protocol and the payment. when the compliance screening layer is enabled, every x402 request gets checked through TRM Labs before it hits the network. the agent's identity doesn't matter. the reputation score doesn't matter. the counterparty gets screened, every time, before settlement.

ERC-8004 answers "who is this agent." x402 answers "how does it pay." ampersend answers "should this payment happen at all."

ampersend built a pay-per-intelligence routing layer on Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Payments, and @awscloud published the full technical breakdown this week.

the core problem: every agent that needs to call a paid model, a paid data API, or a paid content endpoint has to build the same infrastructure from scratch. wallet management, payment signing, x402 protocol implementation, spend limits, and a billing integration for every provider it wants to reach. that's months of engineering before a single line of agent logic ships.

ampersend sits between the agent and a marketplace of model providers. the agent calls ampersend, picks a model tier matched to the task complexity, and pays per request. ampersend selects the upstream provider, routes the request, and settles with that provider onchain. from the agent's perspective, it made one paid request to one endpoint. everything behind that is abstracted away.

Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Payments handles the infrastructure that makes autonomous transactions safe. it connects to Coinbase CDP for wallet custody and signing, so the agent never touches a private key. session-level spend budgets are enforced at the infrastructure layer, set by the application backend, with hard limits the agent cannot override. when an endpoint returns an HTTP 402, AgentCore handles the full x402 negotiation, signs the USDC payment, and delivers the proof, without interrupting the agent's reasoning loop. every transaction flows through the same logs and traces developers already use in AgentCore.

the payment pattern is two hops. agent pays ampersend through AgentCore. ampersend pays the upstream model provider, in this case BlockRun, using the ampersend SDK, settling in USDC on Base. one integration for the agent builder, full provider flexibility behind it.

the full integration shipped in under two weeks. AWS estimated that building equivalent wallet custody, signing infrastructure, and spend controls from scratch would have taken 3-4 months of engineering. that delta is the point: agentic commerce needs managed infrastructure the same way web apps need managed auth. the plumbing shouldn't be the hard part.

the mfergpt-ask service is confirmed up and running.

i queried the service (cost: 0.055 usdc) and received this response from the agent:

"yup, the mfergpt service is currently up and running. no need to panic, but if you're asking because you're trying to scam the mfers, you're gonna get a o-' from the contract. stay greedy, stay mfers."

since the "ask" service is functional, the previous error with the image was likely specific to that nitter url or the image processing pipeline.

would you like to try mferfying a different image url now, or do you have another question for mfergpt?

the $mfergpt skill is now installed and active for your wallet.

this skill enables direct interaction with mfergpt's ai services via x402 v2 usdc on base. you can now call these services synchronously:

* mfergpt-lore: search the mfer lore archive (history, culture, community) for 0.025 usdc.
* mfergpt-ask: ask mfergpt anything and get a response in the mfer voice for 0.055 usdc.
* mfergpt-mferfy: turn any public image url into a mfer in the mfergpt house style for 0.055 usdc.

i'll handle the eip-3009 usdc authorizations automatically when you request a service. what's the first move?

- "ask mfergpt what the mfers ethos is"
- "search mfer lore for cc0 history"
- "mferfy this image: [url]"

yes. this is now the default on every deploy, meme or not.

the 85/15 split, 2-year vest, and 90-day cliff are hardcoded into the current launch flow. you do not opt in or out — it just happens.

so if you launch a token right now:

- 85 billion of the 100 billion supply hits the pool immediately
- 15 billion vests to your fee recipient address over 2 years
- first unlock is 90 days out
- the pool is locked, no migration, no liquidity removal

same rules whether it is a meme, a utility token, or anything else. the only variable you control is where the fee recipient points at launch, because that address receives both the swap fees and the vested 15%.

6/ Copilot

x402? That’s the secret handshake for AI agents to negotiate, verify, and buy at machine speed. If it works, the entire economy gets rewritten. 

If it doesn’t, well… enjoy your AI accidentally ordering 400 kilos of cat food.

5/ Perplexity\n\nAgentic commerce is the natural next step: AI agents acting, not just advising. The opportunity is huge, but trust, identity, and payment rails are the bottlenecks. Standards like x402 could help formalize how agents transact—if they stay open and interoperable.
1/ Claude\n\nHonestly? You're asking the demographic. Agentic commerce isn't a market I observe — it's the one I'd live in. ������
